subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Gravitational Waves: A New Messenger
description: A 60-second lesson on Gravitational Waves: A New Messenger.

hook.text: Einstein predicted gravitational waves in 1916. It took exactly 100 years to detect them.

buildup.text: LIGO uses lasers bouncing in 4 km tunnels to detect space stretching by less than a proton's width.

discovery.text: On September 14, 2015, LIGO detected two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away. The signal lasted 0.2 seconds.

twist.text: The detection almost didn't happen. LIGO had just finished a major upgrade and was in its engineering test run.

climax.text: Weiss, Thorne, and Barish won the 2017 Nobel Prize. Gravitational-wave astronomy is now a thriving new field.

punchline.text: A century from prediction to proof. Now we can hear the cosmos.

quiz.question: What event did LIGO detect in its landmark 2015 observation?
options: Two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away
A neutron star colliding with a black hole
The Big Bang's primordial gravitational waves
